entry.title: More Power, New Name: The UI Flexibility Service Explained
entry.content:
SAPUI5 flexibility for adapting standard SAP apps

SAPUI5 is SAP’s strategic Web framework for SAP Fiori. SAPUI5 flexibility allows developers, key users, and end users to adapt standard apps with simple drag-and-drop tools. Discover all the capabilities here: SAPUI5 Flexibility - All You Need to Know.

 

Key user adaptation

Key user adaptation is built into SAP Fiori Launchpad / SAP Build Work Zone and is included commercially as part of the respective product. Outside these products, the same capabilities are available as a dedicated service, UI5 flexibility for key users, on SAP BTP, enabling key user adaptation for standalone apps, for example, for SAP partners building their own apps. The service is available via BTPEA, BTP Trial, and partner TD&D licenses. See details in the SAP Discovery Center.

 

What's new?

Some time ago, we added the end user personalization capability to the UI5 flexibility for key users service. This allows also business users to easily change the UI of standard apps, helping them work more efficiently. This created confusion, as the service name UI5 flexibility for key users no longer reflects the full range of benefits. We therefore decided to rename the service:

"UI5 flexibility for key users" ➔ "UI flexibility service"

You will see the name being changed now across all SAP assets, e.g.:

 

Apart from the name, nothing changes in the scope or functionality of the UI flexibility service.

entry.title: Partner Learning Accelerator: SAP certified - SAP Fiori application developer
entry.content:
Want to pass the SAP Fiori Application Developer certification faster?

Join this 6-hour Partner Learning Accelerator and focus on what actually matters for the C_FIORD exam.

  • SAPUI5 basics.
  • Fiori architecture.
  • List Report and Object Page.
  • Data binding, controllers, extensibility.

No fluff.  Clear guidance.  Real examples.

The idea is simple.  You already know parts of Fiori.  Now connect everything and fill the gaps before the exam.  If you have worked with Business Application Studio and touched JavaScript, HTML, or CSS, you are ready.

Short session.  High impact.  Immediate value for your projects.

Seats are filling.  Register now.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/apr/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00013653.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #SAPCertification #SAPUI5 #TechSkills

entry.title: CSV Validator App Is Now Open Source
entry.content:
From Solution to Community Tool

A few weeks ago, I shared how CSVValidationApp helps solve data quality issues in SAP SuccessFactors Talent Intelligence Hub. The response surprised me. Many have built similar workarounds, spent hours cleaning data manually, or watched perfectly good imports fail because of stray HTML tags.

That's when I felt this shouldn't be "my tool." It should be our tool.

Today, CSV Validator App is officially open source and available on GitHub under the Apache 2.0 license: github.com/SAP/csv-validator

Why This Matters

I didn't build this tool because I love CSV files (I don't). I built it because I was tired of seeing good work derailed by preventable issues. An HR team prepares a skills library update, exports clean data, imports it into TIH—and suddenly descriptions are broken, special characters are corrupted, and they're troubleshooting instead of enabling their workforce.

It's frustrating. And it's common.

Making this open source isn't about recognition or downloads. It's about honesty: I don't have all the answers. You might be importing recruiting data with different quirks. You might support a region with specific character encoding needs. You might have found a validation rule I missed.

By opening the code, we can solve these problems together—transparently, collaboratively, and faster than any of us could alone.

What You're Getting

This is a complete SAP Fiori application you can run today:

Core Validation:

  • Detects encoding issues (UTF-8 compliance, BOM, null bytes)
  • Validates CSV structure (headers, delimiters, column consistency)
  • Identifies problematic content (HTML tags, entities, control characters, special characters)
  • Checks field lengths and data integrity

Auto-Correction:

  • Strips HTML tags while preserving content
  • Converts HTML entities to UTF-8 characters (  → space, ü → ü)
  • Properly escapes special characters
  • Generates a clean, download-ready corrected file

UI Features:

  • Drag-and-drop file upload
  • Interactive preview of your CSV
  • Filterable table showing issues by row and type
  • Side-by-side view of original vs. corrected content
  • One-click download of the corrected file

Privacy:

  • Everything runs locally in your browser
  • No uploads, no servers, no data leaves your machine

It's built with SAP UI5, follows SAP Fiori standards, and runs via npm. Five minutes from clone to validation.

Getting Started

git clone https://github.com/SAP/csv-validator.git
cd csv-validator
npm install
npm start

Open your browser to the app url, drop in a CSV file, and click Validate. That's it.
You can test by uploading your csv and press validate. Here is a sample screenshot of what you can expect:

 

Where We Go From Here

I have ideas for improvements—support for other SuccessFactors modules, batch processing, direct API integration. But I'm more interested in hearing yours.

If you use this tool and it helps, tell me what's missing. If it doesn't catch an issue it should, open a GitHub issue with an example. If you have a fix or enhancement, submit a pull request. If you just want to share your experience, leave a comment below.

This isn't a finished product. It's a starting point. I'm learning as much from your use cases as you might learn from the code.

An Honest Ask

If you work with SuccessFactors data imports—whether you're an administrator, consultant, or developer—try this tool with your real files. Not a sanitized demo file. Your actual data (with sensitive info removed, of course).

Then tell me:

  • Did it catch issues you would have missed?
  • Did it flag false positives that aren't actually problems?
  • What validation rule would make your life easier?

And if you've built something similar or have ideas for collaboration, let's talk. The goal isn't to centralize everything into this one tool—it's to raise the baseline for data quality across the community.

Resources:

  • GitHub Repository: github.com/SAP/csv-validator
  • Original Blog Post: Bridging Data Quality Gaps in TIH with CSVValidationApp
  • Issues & Feedback: github.com/SAP/csv-validator/issues
  • Contribution Guidelines: github.com/SAP/csv-validator/CONTRIBUTING.md

Data quality shouldn't be a bottleneck. Let's make it a strength—together.

entry.title: How to Integrate UI5 Test Coverage with SonarQube in SAP UI5 Projects
entry.content:
When working with SAP UI5 applications, automated tests are already a good practice. But once the project grows, test execution alone is no longer enough. Teams want to see how much of the application is actually covered by tests, especially in pull requests and CI/CD pipelines.

In this post, I will show how I implemented test coverage reporting for a SAP UI5 application and integrated it with SonarQube so that coverage metrics are visible directly in the quality dashboard.

The solution is based on:

  • ui5-test-runner for running QUnit tests with coverage enabled
  • nyc for generating LCOV reports
  • a custom normalization step to include untested files
  • SonarQube for importing and displaying coverage metrics

This setup works especially well in CI pipelines and helps teams track both coverage on new code and overall project coverage.

Why This Is Needed

In many UI5 projects, unit tests exist, but coverage is not published anywhere centrally. As a result:

  • developers do not see how well new changes are covered
  • pull requests cannot be validated against coverage quality gates
  • SonarQube shows no meaningful UI coverage data
  • uncovered source files may be silently ignored

The main challenge is that in UI5 projects, coverage collection is not always straightforward when tests are executed in the browser. Even if coverage is enabled, the generated report may contain only files that were actually loaded during test execution. This creates an incomplete picture for SonarQube.

To solve this, I implemented an approach that not only generates LCOV coverage but also normalizes it so that all relevant source files are represented, including files with 0% coverage.

Solution Overview

The final flow looks like this:

  1. ui5-test-runner runs QUnit tests with coverage instrumentation
  2. nyc generates the LCOV report
  3. a custom script normalizes the report and adds missing source files
  4. SonarQube imports the LCOV file and displays the coverage metrics

This gives a reliable and CI-friendly setup for SAP UI5 applications.

Prerequisites

Install the required development dependencies:

npm install --save-dev ui5-test-runner nyc puppeteer

What each package is used for

Package Purpose
ui5-test-runnerRuns UI5/QUnit tests and supports code coverage
nycIstanbul-based coverage engine used under the hood
puppeteerRuns tests in headless Chrome, useful for CI

Step 1. Configure ui5-test-runner

Create a ui5-test-runner.json file with the basic setup:

{
 "pageTimeout": 60000,
 "coverage": true,
 "coverageReportDir": "coverage",
 "reportDir": "report",
 "parallel": 2
}

What this configuration does

  • enables coverage collection
  • stores coverage artifacts in the coverage folder
  • defines a timeout for browser-based execution
  • allows parallel test execution for better performance

Step 2. Configure NYC

Even though ui5-test-runner handles much of the work, it relies on NYC internally.
That is why adding an explicit .nycrc.json file is important.

{
 "all": true,
 "sourceMap": false,
 "reporter": ["lcov", "text-summary"],
 "report-dir": "./coverage",
 "temp-dir": "./.nyc_output",
 "include": ["webapp/**/*.js"],
 "exclude": ["webapp/test/**", "webapp/localService/**"],
 "cwd": "."
}

Important points here

  • all: true is important because we want full project visibility
  • test sources are excluded from business code coverage
  • lcov is required because this is the format SonarQube imports

Step 3. Configure SonarQube

Now we need to tell SonarQube where the UI5 sources, tests, and LCOV report are located.

Create or update sonar-project.properties:

 
sonar.projectKey=your-project-key
sonar.projectName=your-project-name

# Source configuration
sonar.sources=webapp/
sonar.exclusions=webapp/localService/**,**/node_modules/**

# Test configuration
sonar.tests=webapp/test/
sonar.test.inclusions=webapp/test/**/*.js

# Coverage report path
sonar.javascript.lcov.reportPaths=coverage/lcov.info
sonar.coverage.exclusions=webapp/test/**,webapp/localService/**

# Encoding
sonar.sourceEncoding=UTF-8

At this point, the most important property is:

sonar.javascript.lcov.reportPaths=coverage/lcov.info

This is the file SonarQube reads to compute the coverage metrics.

Step 4. Add NPM Scripts

The next step is to automate local and CI execution.

In package.json:

{
"scripts": {
"test": "ui5 serve --config ui5-test.yaml --port 8080 & SERVER_PID=$!; sleep 3; ui5-test-runner --url http://localhost:8080/test/unit/unitTests.qunit.html --coverage --coverage-proxy; TEST_EXIT=$?; kill $SERVER_PID 2>/dev/null || true; exit $TEST_EXIT",
"test:ci": "ui5 serve --config ui5-test.yaml --port 8080 & SERVER_PID=$!; sleep 3; ui5-test-runner --url http://localhost:8080/test/unit/unitTests.qunit.html --coverage --coverage-proxy --ci; TEST_EXIT=$?; kill $SERVER_PID 2>/dev/null || true; npm run coverage:normalize; exit $TEST_EXIT",
"coverage:normalize": "node scripts/normalize-coverage.js"
}
}

Why --coverage-proxy is important

When UI5 tests are executed through a served application URL, --coverage-proxy enables on-the-fly instrumentation. Without it, coverage may be missing or incomplete.

Key flags

Flag Meaning
--coverageEnables coverage collection
--coverage-proxyInstruments files dynamically in browser-based execution
--ciUses CI-friendly non-interactive output

Step 5. Normalize Coverage for Untested Files

This was the most important part of the implementation.

The problem

When --coverage-proxy is used, only files that are actually loaded during the test run are instrumented.
That means if a source file is never loaded, it may not appear in lcov.info at all.

For SonarQube, this is problematic because missing files are not always treated the same way as explicitly uncovered files. As a result, the reported coverage can be misleading.

The fix

I added a normalization script that:

  1. scans all source files in webapp
  2. checks which files are already present in lcov.info
  3. adds missing source files with 0% coverage
  4. normalizes paths so that they match SonarQube expectations

Create scripts/normalize-coverage.js:

const fs = require('fs');
const path = require('path');

const lcovPath = 'coverage/lcov.info';
const webappDir = 'webapp';

// Get all JS source files (excluding test and localService)
function getAllSourceFiles() {
const files = [];
function walk(dir) {
const entries = fs.readdirSync(dir, { withFileTypes: true });
for (const entry of entries) {
const fullPath = path.join(dir, entry.name);
if (entry.isDirectory()) {
if (entry.name !== 'test' && entry.name !== 'localService' && entry.name !== 'node_modules') {
walk(fullPath);
}
} else if (entry.name.endsWith('.js')) {
const relativePath = path.relative(webappDir, fullPath).replace(/\\/g, '/');
files.push(relativePath);
}
}
}
walk(webappDir);
return files;
}

// Parse existing lcov.info to get covered files
function parseLcov(content) {
const covered = new Set();
for (const line of content.split('\n')) {
if (line.startsWith('SF:')) {
covered.add(line.slice(3));
}
}
return covered;
}

// Generate lcov entry for uncovered file
function generateUncoveredEntry(filePath) {
const fullPath = path.join(webappDir, filePath);
if (!fs.existsSync(fullPath)) return null;

const content = fs.readFileSync(fullPath, 'utf8');
const lines = content.split('\n');
const executableLines = [];

for (let i = 0; i < lines.length; i++) {
const line = lines[i].trim();
if (line &&
!line.startsWith('//') &&
!line.startsWith('/*') &&
!line.startsWith('*') &&
line !== '{' &&
line !== '}' &&
line !== '});' &&
line !== ']);' &&
line !== '};' &&
line !== '"use strict";') {
executableLines.push(i + 1);
}
}

if (executableLines.length === 0) return null;

let entry = `SF:${filePath}\n`;
entry += `FNF:0\nFNH:0\n`;
for (const lineNum of executableLines) {
entry += `DA:${lineNum},0\n`;
}
entry += `LF:${executableLines.length}\nLH:0\n`;
entry += `BRF:0\nBRH:0\n`;
entry += `end_of_record\n`;

return entry;
}

// Main
if (!fs.existsSync(lcovPath)) {
console.log('No lcov.info found, skipping');
process.exit(0);
}

let content = fs.readFileSync(lcovPath, 'utf8');
const lines = content.split('\n');
const normalized = [];

// Normalize paths (remove webapp/ prefix to match sonar.sources)
for (const line of lines) {
if (line.startsWith('SF:')) {
let filePath = line.slice(3);
const webappMatch = filePath.match(/[/\\]?webapp[/\\](.+)$/);
if (webappMatch) {
filePath = webappMatch[1];
}
filePath = filePath.replace(/\\/g, '/').replace(/^\//, '');
normalized.push('SF:' + filePath);
} else {
normalized.push(line);
}
}

// Get already covered files
const coveredFiles = parseLcov(normalized.join('\n'));

// Add uncovered files
const allFiles = getAllSourceFiles();
let uncoveredContent = '';

for (const file of allFiles) {
if (!coveredFiles.has(file)) {
const entry = generateUncoveredEntry(file);
if (entry) uncoveredContent += entry;
}
}

fs.writeFileSync(lcovPath, normalized.join('\n') + '\n' + uncoveredContent);
console.log('Coverage normalized for SonarQube');

A Critical Detail: Path Mapping Between LCOV and SonarQube

This part is easy to overlook, but it is often the real reason why SonarQube shows 0% coverage even when the LCOV file exists.

If SonarQube is configured with:

sonar.sources=webapp/

then SonarQube treats webapp/ as the root of the source tree.

That means:

  • webapp/model/messages.js is indexed by SonarQube as model/messages.js
  • therefore lcov.info must contain:
 
SF:model/messages.js
and not:
SF:webapp/model/messages.js

In other words

The path in SF: must be relative to sonar.sources.

That is exactly why the normalization script rewrites the paths.

CI/CD Integration

Once the local setup works, integrating it into the pipeline is straightforward.

SAP Piper example

In .pipeline/config.yml:

steps:
 npmExecuteScripts:
  install: true
  virtualFrameBuffer: false
  dockerImage: 'cypress/browsers:node-20.18.0-chrome-130.0.6723.69-1-ff-131.0.3-edge-130.0.2849.52-1'
  runScripts:
   - 'test:ci'
  sonarExecuteScan:
  serverUrl: 'https://sonar.tools.sap'

stages:
 Build:
  npmExecuteScripts: true
  sonarExecuteScan: true

GitHub Actions example

- name: Run tests with coverage
run: npm run test:ci

- name: SonarQube Scan
uses: sonarqube-scan-action@v1
with:
args: >
-Dsonar.projectKey=${{ env.PROJECT_KEY }}

Generated Coverage Artifacts

After running:

npm run test:ci

 

you should see:

coverage/
├── lcov.info
├── lcov-report/
│ └── index.html
└── cobertura-coverage.xml

 

What these files are used for

  • lcov.info → imported by SonarQube
  • lcov-report/ → human-readable HTML report for local inspection
  • cobertura-coverage.xml → optional integration with other tools

How Coverage Appears in SonarQube

After the scan, SonarQube usually shows two metrics that are especially important:

Metric Meaning
Coverage on New CodeCoverage only for changed lines in the branch or PR
Overall CoverageCoverage across the entire project

This distinction matters a lot.

A project can have:

  • high coverage on new code, because the changed lines were properly tested
  • low overall coverage, because legacy parts of the application still have poor test coverage

That is not a contradiction. It is normal SonarQube behavior.

Troubleshooting

SonarQube shows 0% coverage

Check the following first:

  1. coverage/lcov.info is generated successfully
  2. sonar.javascript.lcov.reportPaths points to the correct file
  3. SF: entries match the structure expected by sonar.sources

Useful check:

grep "^SF:" coverage/lcov.info

Some files are missing from coverage

This usually happens because only loaded files were instrumented during the browser run.

That is exactly why the normalization step is needed.

Without normalization, SonarQube may import only a partial coverage picture.

Coverage on new code is high, but overall coverage is low

This is expected and does not necessarily indicate a problem.

It simply means:

  • your recent changes are well tested
  • older application code still lacks coverage

Warning: “Unable to process all coverage”

This may appear when using ui5-test-runner with --coverage-proxy.

In practice, this warning means that not all source files could be discovered automatically during runtime instrumentation.

The custom normalization script compensates for this by adding the missing files manually.

Final Thoughts

For me, the key goal of this implementation was not just to execute UI5 tests with coverage enabled, but to make the results usable and trustworthy inside SonarQube.

The most important lessons from this setup were:

  • generating LCOV is only the first step
  • path alignment between LCOV and sonar.sources is critical
  • browser-based runtime instrumentation does not guarantee full project visibility
  • normalizing missing files makes the SonarQube result much more accurate

With this approach in place, SAP UI5 projects can benefit from the same quality visibility that backend projects usually already have: clear coverage reports, PR metrics, and enforceable quality gates.

References

  • ui5-test-runner Documentation
  • SonarQube JavaScript Coverage Documentation
  • LCOV Format Specification

entry.title: Blank PDF issue in SAP Fiori apps: Fix
entry.content:
Hi All

During my work with SAP UI5 applications, I newly faced an issue where PDF files won't open and instead displayed an empty screen in the browser which avoids users from accessing/ printing required documents. Upon analyzing, I identified that certain browser settings in Edge and Chrome were causing the issue.

Fix: 

To fix the blank PDF screen issue, I modified below settings in Edge and Chrome. These changes helped PDFs are managed to open within the browser environment.

Edge:

Chrome:

In the Site Settings, check if Pop-ups are allowed (if not, change the setting)

(old site settings screenshot)

After updating these changes, the PDF documents opened rightly without an empty screen. These settings assist the browser process and display PDF content from SAP UI5 apps.

The blank screen issue when opening PDFs in SAP UI5 applications can relate to browser configuration than the application itself. By adjusting the necessary settings in Microsoft Edge and Google Chrome, the issue can be fixed. Also, these changes are not limited to SAP UI5 and can be applied to other web applications facing similar PDF display issues.

Note: This blog post is originally written based on my personal experience and knowledge. I have ensured that the content reflects my own understanding.

Thank you!!
BR// Dhanasupriya Sidagam

entry.title: Celebrating my 300th blog post on the SAP Community
entry.content:
I noticed that my last blog post was my 300th on the SAP Community. In those 300 posts, I have covered many topics related to user experience, clustered into a few key areas. To celebrate this milestone, I’ll reflect on how the topics I covered mirrored the evolution of user experience at SAP, what I learned from the community, what’s next, and end with a call to action for you.

My 300 blogs posts contain a historical account on the evolution of SAP’s user experience

Product Marketing is much easier with great products

Before going into the content, let me acknowledge that there is no product marketing without product. All these blog posts about our UX products would not have happened without the amazing development, product management, design, QA, documentation, and other colleagues that build and test these solutions. I’m grateful to the many coworkers over the years who came up with ideas to solve real customer pain points regarding SAP’s user experience. I also appreciate the many customers and partners that provided ideas for blogs posts in online forums, SAP events, and user groups.

The topics I covered mirrored the evolution of user experience at SAP

In my product marketing role, most of my posts fell into one of these broad categories:

  • Product
  • Strategy
  • Events
  • Customer and partner enablement

The topic mix shifted over time as our team expanded the products we worked on.

Product

  • Release announcements.
    Product-focused posts generally followed the cadence of our release cycles. This shifted from annual or semi-annual service packs for on-prem solutions to continuous innovation / continuous delivery pipelines for our cloud-based tools. My first five years of posts were on SAP Screen Personas. From version 1.0’s first appearance at TechEd 2012 through the release announcements of version 3 SP18, after which Rohith Deraje took over for SP19 and beyond.
    SAP Fiori elements and SAP Fiori tools were next to join our team’s portfolio. I posted some of the early announcements for those products until Katrin Polloczek, Christoph Gollmick, Ashley Tung, and Ian Quigley took ownership of the content.
    Collaborative ERP was a more recent addition to SAP UX Foundation. I posted the early announcements on our strategy and early versions until Regina Sheynblat picked this up.
    Embedded AI in SAP Fiori apps is the newest part of our portfolio, with Conrad Bernal now posting about new innovations and beta opportunities.
  • Roadmap updates – when you have a product that 1000s of customers use to run their business, they want to know what’s coming next. Towards that end, I drafted many roadmap updates over the years on the various solutions in our portfolio.
  • Frequently asked questions (FAQ) documents – one of the most interesting parts of my role is interacting with customers in various forums and hearing their questions. Often, we hear the same questions from multiple people. In those cases, it’s worth capturing them into an FAQ document.
  • Technical tips and tricks – as part of the product team, many customers reach out vie email to ask questions that are more detailed than can be covered in an FAQ document. If their inquiry is relevant to a broader audience, I put together tips they can use to get started on a project or break through a barrier they are facing and post it so others in the SAP Community can learn.
  • Customer and partner success stories – sometimes our customers want to share their success with SAP UX solutions. In those cases, I have helped them tell their story through the SAP Community.

Strategy

When I looked through my blog posts over time, they followed the same evolution of SAP’s broader UX strategy. We built SAP Screen Personas to improve the SAP GUI user experience, one screen at a time. SAP Fiori elements accomplished this at scale, for the most common business processes. Then we rolled out SAP Fiori tools to make it easier to build Fiori elements apps. Then collaborative ERP to help people team up to solve complex business problems. Most recently, we added AI to both the Fiori apps that we ship and the tools we (and you) use to build them.

Posts on UX strategy explain how all the pieces fit together so customers can plan their digital transformation, SAP S/4HANA migration, or other strategic initiative knowing where SAP is heading. Since customers are not concerned about our internal organizational structures, I blogged on solutions within the UX Foundation portfolio as well as adjacent solutions such as SAP Build, BTP, and Joule. Sometimes we include a peek behind the curtain, so people understand how SAP Fiori powers the apps in SAP S/4HANA and what to expect as we continue to add both simplicity and power to our flagship SAP Cloud ERP solution.

Events

It’s natural to blog about events to help people set the right expectations, plan their agendas, and make the most of their investment in attending the event. Typically, I like to highlight the sessions most relevant to the customers and partners that follow me on the SAP Community and LinkedIn.

For Sapphire 2026, I have a list of recommended sessions on AI and UX, along with what I’m planning with Nicole Smythe on bridging the skills gap to accelerate your AI projects.

My other posts have covered SAP TechEd, ASUG Tech Connect, ASUG chapter meetings, and, of course, our own SAP UX Innovation Day events. Next one is on June 11 in Palo Alto, if you are interested in attending.

Customer and partner enablement

I also noticed a number of blog posts on various training courses for customers, partners, and colleagues. In the early days, we created several openSAP training courses. The ones that are still relevant have moved to learning.sap.com. We also have posts on hands-on exercises from other events or tutorials from our development experts. It makes sense that people appreciate training from the team that builds and supports the products.

The SAP community is for learning, connecting, and listening

I would be remiss if I did not reflect on the SAP Community and the role it played in my personal growth at SAP. I learned so much from the questions and comments that people submitted, and through my interactions with the highly professional SAP Community team.

Much more than the number of blog posts I have written, I’m most proud of how many other colleagues I inspired to start blogging; that list continues to grow.

And thanks to my long-time friend, former colleague, and co-author, Michael Procopio, for getting me started on my blogging journey back in our days at HP Software.

The next UI will build from our Fiori experience and use AI heavily

While SAP Fiori solves many issues, we are still working to simplify how customers consume SAP and developers create apps. Needless to say, AI will play an increasingly important role in both of these dimensions. Your ongoing feedback will continue to drive our development priorities.
You will hear more about this at Sapphire and in our next SAP UX Innovation Day.

In addition to providing your feedback through the SAP Community, you are welcome to join our monthly SAP Developer Roundtable to share your thoughts on developing SAP Fiori apps using our latest tools and technologies.
If you are a public cloud customer, you can join our online user group to discuss UX and adjacent topics with your peers and SAP experts.

 

We will continue to address your concerns about usability in SAP Cloud ERP.

To count to 300, start with 1: What are you waiting for?

A much bigger milestone than blog 300 is blog #1.
What are you waiting for? There’s no time like the present!
The SAP Community needs your voice. If you want to brainstorm ideas or need help outlining your first post, please let me know. Like the rest of the SAP Community team, I’m here to help.

Thanks for reading all my stuff over the years.

entry.title: Handling Cross-App Navigation from Flat to Hierarchical Models in Fiori Elements
entry.content:
Cross-app navigation in SAP Fiori Elements (CAP, OData V4) can become tricky when the source and target applications follow different data models. 

In our case, we had a scenario where a flat source application needed to navigate to a hierarchical target application using intent-based navigation.

• Source App: Flat entity model, exposing fields from multiple levels of Target App's hierarchy in a single entity. Navigation source: List Report
• Target App: Hierarchical entity model, top-to-bottom. Navigation target: Object Page

Goal: A row selection in the Source App's List Report triggers intent-based navigation to the corresponding Object Page in the Target App.

 

Standard Setup

  • Semantic Object annotation on Source App's entity
annotate service.SourceAppEntity with @(Common: {SemanticKey: [ID]});
  •  Outbound navigation in Source App's  manifest.json
"crossNavigation": {
    "outbounds": {
        "TargetApp-manage": {
          "semanticObject": "BusinessObject",
          "action": "manage"
        }
    }
}
  • Inbound navigation in TargetApp's manifest.json
"crossNavigation": {
    "inbounds": {
        "TargetApp-manage": {
            "semanticObject": "BusinessObject",
            "action": "manage",
            "signature": {       
              "parameters": {},
              "additionalParameters": "allowed"
            }
        }
     }
}

 

The Problem

With the above configuration in place, the navigation was triggered but failed to resolve correctly to the target Object Page. Inspecting the navigation call in the browser showed that the FLP was receiving the entire binding context of the selected row as navigation parameters.

Since the 'Source App' is a flat entity, this included:

  • Fields from multiple hierarchy levels
  • Fields not present in Target App's entity

The Launchpad attempts to resolve navigation using all provided parameters. When those parameters don’t align with the target entity, resolution becomes unreliable.

The semantic key (ID), the only required parameter was effectively buried among irrelevant fields.

 

Root Cause

This is a data model mismatch:

  • Source App is intentionally broad (flat) to surface data across levels
  • Target App is narrow (hierarchical) and expects a specific key

By default, Fiori Elements passes the full row context and does not account for this mismatch.

 

Solution: @ui.ExcludeFromNavigationContext

The UI vocabulary provides an annotation specifically for this [1] : @ui.ExcludeFromNavigationContext

Applied to a property, it tells the Fiori Elements framework to exclude that field from the navigation context payload when intent-based navigation is triggered.

Apply it to every field in Source App's entity except the semantic key:

annotate service.SourceAppEntity with {
    field1  @ui.ExcludeFromNavigationContext;
    field2  @ui.ExcludeFromNavigationContext;
    field3  @ui.ExcludeFromNavigationContext;
    .
    .
     // repeat for all fields except ID
};

With this in place, the navigation context carries only the ID, which matches the Target App’s inbound signature and allows the Object Page to resolve correctly

 

Key Takeaway

When navigating from a flat source model to a hierarchical target model, this leads to unnecessary parameters being included in the navigation payload. @ui.ExcludeFromNavigationContext allows explicit control so that only the semantic key is passed to the target application.

entry.title: SAP CAPM Project Lifecycle: From Development to Fiori Preview
entry.content:
Let's see an end  to end demonstration for creating the CAPM project, entity, service with annotations, create the entry via Post man tool and Preview the Application in Fiori.

Below mentioned detailed points covered in this Blog,

  1. BTP Cockpit and its properties
  2. Creation of CAP Project
  3. Linkage of Entity to the HANA DB in the SAP HANA Cloud central
  4. Post man tool Authorization and Post Operation
  5. Preview in the Fiori Application

BTP Cockpit and its properties:

Create a BTP Cock PIT Trial account and Sub Account.

In the Instances and Subscription Add SAP HANA Cloud and SAP Business Application Studio.

Create a Space in the Cloud Foundry, which is a shared, isolated location with an organization (org) used to develop, deploy and manage application and services.

In SAP BTP Cockpit administrators navigate to security->Role collections, create or edit a collection, and add roles-derived from application templates-to define the granular access.

Creation of CAP Project:

In this Business Application studio create a Dev space and create a Project as mentioned below.

 

 

 

Once Project created a project with workspace created. Here I have created ENTITY_CRUD project. As per below image under DB Node we can create the entity and service for the entity create under SRV node.

gen

Auto-generated folder created during build; contains compiled artifacts from your CDS models. You normally don’t edit this manually.

 .vscode

Holds VS Code-specific settings like launch configs and workspace preferences for development.

app

Used for UI/front-end apps (like SAP Fiori); contains HTML, UI5, or other frontend resources.

db

Defines your data model and database artifacts using CDS files (like itemrecordsdb.cds) and deployment configs (undeploy.json).

gen

Another generated folder used during build/deploy; stores intermediate files created by CAP tools.

mta_archives

Contains packaged .mtar files used for deployment to SAP BTP (multi-target application archives).

node_modules

Stores all installed npm dependencies required for your CAP project.

srv

Defines the service layer (business logic & APIs); CDS files like itemrecordssrv.cds expose entities as OData services.

.cdsrc-private.json

Local configuration file for CAP (like profiles, credentials); usually not committed to Git.

.gitignore

Specifies files/folders (like node_modules) that Git should ignore.

 eslint.config.mjs

Configuration for code linting to maintain code quality and standards.

mta.yaml

Defines how the application is structured and deployed in SAP BTP (modules, resources, dependencies).

package.json

Main project config file listing dependencies, scripts, and metadata.

package-lock.json

Locks exact versions of installed dependencies for consistent builds.

 README.md

Documentation file describing the project, setup, and usage.

An entity with Itemrecords created. Here I have used ‘manage, cuid’   from @sap/cds/manage which will create a UUID key for every record creation.

Create a service for itemrecords db which acts as an interface between your data models and outside the world like UI apps or external systems.

Once entity and service generated in the project deploy the project using cf deploy mta_archives/<file>.mtar bash commands. After successful deployment we can see service instances created in the BTP cockpit as shown below.

To link the created entity in BAS to SAP HANA Cloud create a service key for the <entity>-db instance as shown from the below steps.

Choose Entity_CRUD-db and click on the navigation panel where service key need to create. In this illustration I have created as ENTITY_CRUD-db-key.

Once service key generated, we can see the JSON file in the view parameter. This JSON file needs to upload in the SAP HANA explorer to link the DB.

Linkage of Entity to the HANA DB in the SAP HANA Cloud central:

Navigate to Subscribed SAP HANA CLOUD and create instances which allows you to use in-memory computing for real-time transactional and analytical processing. Once instance is created it should be in Running status which indicates Active, available for SQL connections and able to perform data processing, backups and administrative tasks.

Now, open the SAP HANA database Explorer in the SAP HANA cloud central, click on the + icon where instance type to be chosen as HDI container and choose Apply from JSON string where need to paste the JSON file from the service key entity in the BTP cockpit.

 

 

 

 

Once JSON file applied Entity from the BAS linked to the SAP HANA Cloud central.

 

 

 

Post man tool Authorization and Post Operation:

To Authenticate authorization in the Post Man tool go to <entity>-auth in the BTP cockpit as shown below.

 

 

Here I have navigated to the ENTITY_CRUD-auth where service key SK-ENTITY_CRUD is created once service key created a JSON file generated with client ID, client secret, URL.

 

 

 

 

 

 

Client ID, URL client secret mapped to the Authorization in the post man tool and generate the Access Token.

 

 

 

 

Using post operation can create a data to the entity in the CAP project.

 

 

 

Successful created entries showing in the SAP HANA Cloud central.

 

 Preview in the Fiori Application:

To Preview the data in the Fiori application, In the CAP project search with Open Application Generator and create a List Report page.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Once Application created, In the project terminal using Cds watch –profile hybrid can view the preview of the Application in Fiori

Required Annotations can use in the srv which will be reflect in the Fiori Application.

 

Conclusion
This demonstration showcased the seamless integration between SAP Business Application Studio, SAP HANA Cloud, and the Fiori front-end. By leveraging annotations and the CAP service layer, we successfully created a functional CRUD application with automated UUID management and secure API access. You are now ready to extend this project with custom business logic or complex UI enhancements.

entry.title: SAP Fiori Elements List Report: Implementing Dual Data Views with Flat and Hierarchical Entities
entry.content:
In many SAP Fiori Elements applications, data is modeled hierarchically. Consider a scenario with a 3-level entity structure:

  • Header (parent)
  • Item (child)
  • SubItem (grandchild)

By default, the List Report displays data at the Header level. However, there is a requirement to provide a Detailed view: a flat representation that surfaces fields from all three levels in a single row, without requiring navigation into each record.

Goal:

Segmented button on the List Report that lets the user toggle between:

  • Detailed View: flat structure, fields from all 3 levels in one row
  • Normal View: standard hierarchical view at the Header level

Why this approach

Instead of modifying the existing hierarchical entity, a separate flat view entity allows both representations to coexist without impacting the original data model.

 

Backend: Exposing a Flat View Entity (CAP)

The standard Header entity only holds parent-level fields. To surface child and grandchild fields in a single flat list, a dedicated view entity is exposed in the CAP service.

This entity (HeaderDetailView) is built using a bottom-to-top approach, starting from SubItem and joining upward through Item to Header, making all fields available at a single level.

// In schema.cds

entity HeaderDetailView as select from SubItem {
    // SubItem fields
    subItemField1 as field1,
    subItemField2 as field2,

    // Item fields (via association)
    item.itemField1 as field3
    item.itemField2 as field4

    // Header fields (via association)
    item.header.headerField1 as field5,
    item.header.headerField2 as field6,
    item.header.ID as ID
};

This entity is then exposed in the service:

// In service.cds

service AppService {
    entity Header         as projection on db.Header;
    entity HeaderDetailView as projection on db.HeaderDetailView;
}

 

UI: Wiring Two Entities into One List Report

The UI layer uses annotations and manifest configuration to expose both entities as separate views within the same List Report.

Step 1: Define LineItem annotations for both views

Detailed view: on HeaderDetailView:

//In Annotations.cds file

annotate service.HeaderDetailView with @(UI: { 
    HeaderInfo                       : {
        TypeName      : 'Item',
        TypeNamePlural: 'Items'
    },
    PresentationVarinat #DetailedView: {
        Visualizations: [{$AnnotationPath:'HeaderDetailView/@UI.LineItem#DetailedView'}],
        GroupBy       : [ID],
    },
    PresentationVarinat #NormalView  : {
        Visualizations: [{$AnnotationPath:'Header/@UI.LineItem'}],
    },
    LineItem #DetailedView           : [
        {
            $Type            : 'UI.DataField',
            Value            : ID,
            ![@UI.Importance]: #High
        },
        {
            $Type            : 'UI.DataField',
            Value            : field1,
            ![@UI.Importance]: #High
        },
        //add other fields in similar fashion
    ],
});

Normal view: on Header:

//In Annotations.cds file

annotate service.Header with @(UI: { 
    HeaderInfo                       : {
        TypeName      : 'Item',
        TypeNamePlural: 'Items'
    },
    LineItem          : [
        {
            $Type            : 'UI.DataField',
            Value            : ID,
            ![@UI.Importance]: #High
        },
        {
            $Type            : 'UI.DataField',
            Value            : field1,
            ![@UI.Importance]: #High
        },
        //add other header level-fields in similar fashion
    ],
});

 

Step 2: Define SelectionPresentationVariant on both entities

This is what the List Report uses to derive the tab label and resolve the correct LineItem.

//In Annotations.cds file

annotate service.HeaderDetailView with @(UI: {
    SelectionPresentationVariant #DetailedView: {
        $Type              : 'UI.SelectionPresentationVariantType',
        Text               : 'Detailed View',
        PresentationVariant: {
            Visualizations: ['@UI.LineItem#DetailedView']
        }
    }
});

annotate service.Header with @(UI: { 
    SelectionPresentationVariant #NormalView: {
        $Type              : 'UI.SelectionPresentationVariantType',
        Text               : 'Normal View',
        PresentationVariant: {
            Visualizations: ['@UI.LineItem']
        }
    }
});

 

Step 3: Configure manifest.json

"targets": {
        "HeaderList": {
          "type": "Component",
          "id": "HeaderList",
          "name": "sap.fe.templates.ListReport",
          "options": {
            "settings": {
              "contextPath": "/HeaderDetailView",
              "views": {
                "paths": [
                  {
                    "key": "tab1",
                    "annotationPath": "com.sap.vocabularies.UI.v1.SelectionPresentationVariant#DetailedView"
                  },
                  {
                    "key": "tab2",
                    "entitySet": "ObjectPageEntity",
                    "annotationPath": "com.sap.vocabularies.UI.v1.SelectionPresentationVariant#NormalView"
                  }
                ]

              },
              
              "enhanceI18n": "i18n/i18n.properties",
              "variantManagement": "Page",
              "initialLoad": "Enabled",
              "navigation": {
                "ObjectPageEntity": {
                  "detail": {
                    "route": "ObjectPageEntityRoute"
                  }
                }
              },
              "controlConfiguration": {
                "@com.sap.vocabularies.UI.v1.SelectionPresentationVariant#DetailedView": {
                  "tableSettings": {
                    "type": "ResponsiveTable",
                    "disableCopyToClipboard": true,
                    "enablePaste": false,
                    "personalization": {
                      "column": true,
                      "sort": true,
                      "filter": true,
                      "group": true
                    }
                  },
                  "columns": {
                  }
                },
                "/Header/@com.sap.vocabularies.UI.v1.SelectionPresentationVariant#NormalView": {
                  "tableSettings": {
                    "type": "ResponsiveTable",
                    "disableCopyToClipboard": true,
                    "enablePaste": false,
                    "personalization": {
                      "filter": true,
                      "group":false
                    }
                  },
                  "columns": {
                 }
           }
        },

This configuration enables the List Report to render multiple views backed by different entity sets.

Key points:

  • contextPath points to the flat view entity (HeaderDetailView) as the primary entity
  • Tab2 uses entitySet to point to the hierarchical entity (Header)
  • annotationPath must point to SelectionPresentationVariant (not LineItem), as it provides both the visualization reference and the tab label.

Result

The List Report renders a segmented button on the table toolbar, allowing users to switch between Detailed View and Normal View. Thus, both views are available at the same List Report level, without requiring navigation.

>> Normal View:

>> Detailed View:

Summary

Comparison of Views:

 

Detailed View

Normal View

Entity

HeaderDetailView (flat)

Header (hierarchical)

Structure

Fields from all 3 levels

Parent-level fields only

Annotation

UI.LineItem#DetailedView

UI.LineItem

Variant

SelectionPresentationVariant#DetailedView

SelectionPresentationVariant#NormalView

 

Final takeaway

SAP Fiori Elements List Report supports multiple views backed by different entity sets, not just filtered variants of the same entity. SelectionPresentationVariant acts as the bridge between CDS annotations and manifest configuration, controlling both the tab label and the rendered content.

When to use this approach

  • When a hierarchical model needs a flattened analytical view
  • When users need detailed data without navigating to Object Pages
  • When multiple representations of the same data are required in a single List Report

entry.title: OData V4 Fiori Elements: "KeyPath missing at p.getItemFromContext" when selecting Value Help - Fiori
entry.content:
Hello SAP Experts,

I am facing a persistent UI5 ERROR in an OData V4 Fiori Elements application (based on ABAP RAP).

The Scenario: I have a Root Consumption View with an input Parameter. I have assigned a custom CDS Value Help (Dropdown / #XS) to this parameter. The dropdown successfully opens and displays the data in the Fiori Preview.

The Error: When the user clicks/selects an item from the dropdown list, the slection doesn't happen with the following console error in the browser:

Uncaught (in promise) Error: KeyPath missing at p.getItemFromContext

1. Value Help CDS View (Single Key, Dropdown)

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Value Help for Parameter'
@ObjectModel.resultSet.sizeCategory: #XS  

define view entity ZI_VH_CustomValue
  as select from custom_domain_table
{
      @UI.lineItem: [{ position: 10 }]
        key cast( domain_value as abap.char(1) ) as ValueKey,

      @UI.lineItem: [{ position: 20 }]
      @Semantics.text: true
      domain_text as Description
}
where active_status = 'A'

2. The Consumption View (Short Version)

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Main Business Object'
@Metadata.allowExtensions: true

define root view entity ZC_MainBusinessObject
  with parameters

    @EndUserText.label: 'Custom Filter Parameter'
    @Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{
        entity: { name: 'ZI_VH_CustomValue', element: 'ValueKey' },

        /* Explicit binding to pass the value back to the parameter */
        additionalBinding: [{
            localParameter: 'p_custom_filter',
            element: 'ValueKey',
            usage: #RESULT
        }]
    }]
    p_custom_filter : z_custom_data_element

  as select from ZI_MainBusinessObject
{
  key DocumentId,
      DocumentType,
      PostingDate
}
where PostingDate <= $session.system_date

My Questions:

  1. Is there a known limitation or missing @ui/ @Consumption annotation required to bind a Value Help strictly to a PARAMETER (rather than a standard table field) in OData V4?
  2. Why is the OData V4 framework failing to resolve the context for the selected item despite the explicit localParameter binding?

Any insights or workarounds would be highly appreciated.

Thank you!

entry.title: Managing Content Change Notification Issues During SAP Fiori Launchpad Content Exposure to SAP BTP
entry.content:
With the SAP Fiori launchpad content exposure, you can integrate launchpad content from a product into SAP BTP by using an automatic synchronization. During the automatic synchronization, it may happen that the exposure log receives the following error message “Notification for content channel ‘xyz’ not sent. Manual fetch required” (Message Class: /UI2/CDM3_EXP, Message Number: 399).

As you may not immediately recognize that this issue happened, this blog post provides a way of how you can monitor this type of error message using email notification from SAP Cloud Application Lifecycle Management (SAP Cloud ALM).

In short, in the SAP Cloud ALM Integration & Exception Monitoring app, you do the following:

  1. Enable the ABAP application log for the system          
  2. Add a filter for the error message
  3. Add a notification for the error

As an example, we use an SAP S/4HANA system.

Disclaimer: This article is written from the viewpoint of a consumer of SAP Cloud ALM. If you have SAP Cloud ALM-specific questions, such as how to set up your system in SAP Cloud ALM, please reach out to SAP Cloud ALM directly.

Check the following SAP Help Portal pages to see whether you are affected by this issue:

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

  • Enable Content Change Notifications
  • Display Launchpad Content Exposure Logs

SAP Integrated Business Planning

  • Enable Content Change Notifications
  • Display Launchpad Content Exposure Logs

SAP S/4HANA on-premise

  • Manage Content Change Notifications
  • Launchpad Content Exposure Troubleshooting - Error Analysis   

Prerequisites

Before we can start with the parts specific to launchpad exposure in the SAP Cloud ALM configuration, you set up your system in SAP Cloud ALM.

In addition, please ensure that you have the Integration Architect role. See SAP Cloud ALM overview page.

Enabling ABAP Application Log for Your System

First, you configure your system to collect data for the ABAP Application Log.

  1. In SAP Cloud ALM, in the Operations space, open the Integration & Exception Monitoring app.
  2. Now, let’s add a system tile to the overview: In the upper right corner, choose the Select a Scope icon and filter for your system. Select it in the table and choose Apply.
  3. On the overview page, choose the system tile and from its menu, choose Edit Configuration.
  4. For your system, toggle the Data Collection to On.
  5. For your system, choose the arrow icon to go to the details page.
  6. Activate the ABAP Application Log entry.

Adding a Filter for the Launchpad Content Exposure Error Message

Now, let’s create a filter so that we can monitor when the error message “Notification for content channel ‘xyz’ not sent. Manual fetch required” is written to the ABAP Application Log.

  1. In the same screen as above, for the ABAP Application Log, enter the monitoring and events view.
  2. To add the filter, in the Filters table, choose Add.
  3. Enter a filter name. Then, enter the following filter parameters:

    Parameter

    Operator

    Value

    Object

    Is

    /UI2/BE

    Subobject

    Is

    /UI2/CDM3

    Message ID

    Is

    /UI2/CDM3_EXP

    Message Number

    Is

    399

    Status

    Is

    E


  4. Save the filter by choosing the Save button at the bottom of the dialog.

Adding an Event to Be Notified About an Error

The last step is to create an event so that you get notified once the filter which we have created in the previous step detects the launchpad content exposure error message.

Please note that this event will not only be initiated for the launchpad content exposure error message. If you had configured other ABAP Application Log filters in addition, the event will be initiated as well. The reason is that on the date of publication of this blog post, according to 3452860 - How to setup filters for the category "ABAP application log" in Exception Monitoring of SAP Cloud ALM, it is not possible to add filters to the event itself.

  1. To create the event, navigate to the Events tab and choose Add.
  2. As Event Name, choose Erroneous ABAP Application Log and enter a display name.
  3. Define the actions: Activate Create Alert. For example, you can also configure the event to send an email once there is an error written into the ABAP Application Log.
     
  4. Save your entries.

Checking the Event

In the Integration & Exception Monitoring app, you can view all events that you have created and that were initiated on the Alerting tab.

  1. Choose the Alerting tab.
  2. Once the launchpad content exposure error happens, you can see it in the table

Conclusion:

This blog post covered how to proactively monitor HTTP 401 content change notification failures during SAP Fiori launchpad content exposure to SAP BTP using SAP Cloud ALM's Integration & Exception Monitoring app. You have learned how to enable ABAP Application Log data collection, create a targeted filter for message /UI2/CDM3_EXP 399, and configure automated email notifications to detect synchronization issues before they impact your users. By implementing this monitoring approach, you can transform reactive troubleshooting into proactive incident management, ensuring continuous content availability between your on-premise systems and SAP BTP.

If you have questions about the configuration steps or would like to share your experiences with launchpad content exposure monitoring, please feel free to leave a comment below.

entry.title: Create New Catalog, Space, Page, Section and Role – S4HANA Private Cloud
entry.content:
Like my previous article on classic concept of Fiori Groups in combination with Catalogs, this article is based on single-functional consultant approach where FICO consultants don't have to depend on ABAP or BASIS consultants to achieve overall objective.

We are going to develop a unified Fiori Space, create its relevant page, add related section therein, and then finally assign the respective Fiori tiles / apps.

For smoother learning and easier follow-up, I have devided this article in five main areas:

A. Create New Transport Request 

B. Create New Z Catalog and map it with required Fiori Apps

C. Create New Role and Assign the New Catalog

D. Create New Space, New Page and New Section

E. Assign Fiori Apps to Space, Pages and Sections

So lets start;

A. Create New Transport Request

Go to t-code SE10 and create a new TR where all configuration activities will be saved.

Click  button

Chose the radio button “Customizing Request”

New Transport Request will be created.

B. Create New Z Catalog and map it with required Fiori Apps

  1. In GUI, enter t-code /n/UI2/FLPD_CUST

Following window will open in the browser, this is SAP Fiori Launchpad Designer.

  1. We will create catalog for GL Report F2217 (Display Line Items in General Ledger)

Go to SAP online Fiori Library https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#

and find the app F2217

Go to Tab  and expand the node ‘Configuration’

Find the catalog (either technical or business),

I chose the technical catalog SAP_TC_FIN_ACC_COMMON

  1. Go back to FLP Designer page (shown in step B-1).

Click on button available on right top corner of FLP Designer screen.

Following pop-up window will appear, here select the Transport Request you created in step A.

Click OK button to close the pop-up window. Now all activities will save in this transport request.

  1. Click on button available on left bottom of the screen.

A pop-up window will appear, here provide the Title and ID of new catalog.

Click on save button to create our new catalog.

  1. Now in catalog search field, specify the standard technical catalog SAP_TC_FIN_ACC_COMMON

Here find the app F2217

Click on the button “Tiles” as shown below:

Tile details will appear in table as below:

Select the row and you will notice few buttons have been enabled in bottom tray.

One of these isclick it, and following pop-up window will show:

Here provide the new catalog ID which we created in step B-4,

Select the catalog and SAP will save the reference of desired tile F2217 in our new catalog.

  1. Now on same screen, select the button “Target Mapping”

Here select the appropriate Semantic Object “GLAccount” and Action “displayGLLineItemReportingView” as shown below:

Now click on buttonand following pop-up window will appear:

Here provide the new catalog ID which we created in step B-4,

Select the catalog and SAP will save the reference of Target Mapping in our new catalog.

Now specify the new Catalog ID in search field (as shown below):

Click on Tile and Target Mapping button to confirm the assignment of same semantic object and action.

This confirms the Tile mapping and Target mapping.

We have completed the creation of new Catalog and it’s mapping with the required app F2217.

C. Create New Role and Assign the New Catalog

  1. Go to t-code PFCG and create new role ZFI_GL_REPORTS by clicking the button

     

  2. Click on tab Select Transaction -> SAP Fiori Launchpad -> Launchpad Catalog

     

Following pop-up window will appear,

Here specify the created new catalog ZFI_GL_REPORTS to assign with the role.

Save the data and notice the new catalog assignment.

This completes the catalog assignment to the role.

D. Create New Space, New Page and New Section

  1. Create New Space

On Fiori, access the app “Manage Launchpad Spaces”

Click on button

Following pop-up window will appear, here enter the Space ID, Short description, Space title and specify the TR where space data will be saved.

Click onbutton

On following screen, as shown below, click on button.

Space is now created and saved.

  1. Create New Page

Access the Fiori app “Manage Launchpad Pages”

Following screen will show:

Here click on button.

Following pop-up window will appear, here enter the Page ID, Short description, Page title and specify the TR where space data will be saved.

Click onbutton to create the Page.

Following screen will show:

  1. Create New Section

On Page screen, click on  button.

Following field will appear:

Specify the Section title

Click on button to save the section.

E. Assign Fiori Apps to Space, Pages and Sections

  1. In GUI, go to t-code PFCG and select the role ZFI_GL_REPORTS, we already created in step C-1.

Click on button

Go to tab, and access the path as shown below:

On following pop-up window, assign the Space ZFI_GL_REPORTS

Click on  button

Now click on save button and Space will be assigned to the role.

  1. Assign Page to Space

In Fiori app “Manage Launchpad Spaces”, select the already created space ZFI_GL_REPORTS

Now click on  button

List of available pages will show.

Here find our page ZFI_GL_REPORTS

Select this page by clicking checkbox and then click on button

Click on button.

Page is now assigned to Space.

  1. Assign Fiori App / Tile to Section

Access the Fiori App “Manage Launchpad Pages”

Select the page ZFI_GL_REPORTS

Click on  button

Following screen will appear:

Select the Fiori app “Display Line Items in General Ledger”

and click on  button

Now this Fiori tile will show as assigned to page (as shown below)

Click on  button.

This completes the Fiori tile assignment to Page and section.

Now the required app is visible for user on Fiori interface.

Now you can see the tile under the Space and Page.

Just go to User Homepage and find the Space “GL Reports”

Here you can see the Page and Tile under the same Space.

This concludes the article to buidup the Space, Page, Section and Tile/App assignment.

Hope you have enjoyed the read.

Please do comment for any observations and recommendations.

Best Regards,

entry.title: Create New Catalog, Space, Page, Section and Role – S4HANA Private Cloud
entry.content:
Like my previous article on classic concept of Fiori Groups in combination with Catalogs, this article is based on single-functional consultant approach where FICO consultants don't have to depend on ABAP or BASIS consultants to achieve overall objective.

We are going to develop a unified Fiori Space, create its relevant page, add related section therein, and then finally assign the respective Fiori tiles / apps.

For smoother learning and easier follow-up, I have devided this article in five main areas:

A. Create New Transport Request 

B. Create New Z Catalog and map it with required Fiori Apps

C. Create New Role and Assign the New Catalog

D. Create New Space, New Page and New Section

E. Assign Fiori Apps to Space, Pages and Sections

So lets start;

A. Create New Transport Request

Go to t-code SE10 and create a new TR where all configuration activities will be saved.

Click  button

Chose the radio button “Customizing Request”

New Transport Request will be created.

B. Create New Z Catalog and map it with required Fiori Apps

  1. In GUI, enter t-code /n/UI2/FLPD_CUST

Following window will open in the browser, this is SAP Fiori Launchpad Designer.

  1. We will create catalog for GL Report F2217 (Display Line Items in General Ledger)

Go to SAP online Fiori Library https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#

and find the app F2217

Go to Tab  and expand the node ‘Configuration’

Find the catalog (either technical or business),

I chose the technical catalog SAP_TC_FIN_ACC_COMMON

  1. Go back to FLP Designer page (shown in step B-1).

Click on button available on right top corner of FLP Designer screen.

Following pop-up window will appear, here select the Transport Request you created in step A.

Click OK button to close the pop-up window. Now all activities will save in this transport request.

  1. Click on button available on left bottom of the screen.

A pop-up window will appear, here provide the Title and ID of new catalog.

Click on save button to create our new catalog.

  1. Now in catalog search field, specify the standard technical catalog SAP_TC_FIN_ACC_COMMON

Here find the app F2217

Click on the button “Tiles” as shown below:

Tile details will appear in table as below:

Select the row and you will notice few buttons have been enabled in bottom tray.

One of these isclick it, and following pop-up window will show:

Here provide the new catalog ID which we created in step B-4,

Select the catalog and SAP will save the reference of desired tile F2217 in our new catalog.

  1. Now on same screen, select the button “Target Mapping”

Here select the appropriate Semantic Object “GLAccount” and Action “displayGLLineItemReportingView” as shown below:

Now click on buttonand following pop-up window will appear:

Here provide the new catalog ID which we created in step B-4,

Select the catalog and SAP will save the reference of Target Mapping in our new catalog.

Now specify the new Catalog ID in search field (as shown below):

Click on Tile and Target Mapping button to confirm the assignment of same semantic object and action.

This confirms the Tile mapping and Target mapping.

We have completed the creation of new Catalog and it’s mapping with the required app F2217.

C. Create New Role and Assign the New Catalog

  1. Go to t-code PFCG and create new role ZFI_GL_REPORTS by clicking the button

     

  2. Click on tab Select Transaction -> SAP Fiori Launchpad -> Launchpad Catalog

     

Following pop-up window will appear,

Here specify the created new catalog ZFI_GL_REPORTS to assign with the role.

Save the data and notice the new catalog assignment.

This completes the catalog assignment to the role.

D. Create New Space, New Page and New Section

  1. Create New Space

On Fiori, access the app “Manage Launchpad Spaces”

Click on button

Following pop-up window will appear, here enter the Space ID, Short description, Space title and specify the TR where space data will be saved.

Click onbutton

On following screen, as shown below, click on button.

Space is now created and saved.

  1. Create New Page

Access the Fiori app “Manage Launchpad Pages”

Following screen will show:

Here click on button.

Following pop-up window will appear, here enter the Page ID, Short description, Page title and specify the TR where space data will be saved.

Click onbutton to create the Page.

Following screen will show:

  1. Create New Section

On Page screen, click on  button.

Following field will appear:

Specify the Section title

Click on button to save the section.

E. Assign Fiori Apps to Space, Pages and Sections

  1. In GUI, go to t-code PFCG and select the role ZFI_GL_REPORTS, we already created in step C-1.

Click on button

Go to tab, and access the path as shown below:

On following pop-up window, assign the Space ZFI_GL_REPORTS

Click on  button

Now click on save button and Space will be assigned to the role.

  1. Assign Page to Space

In Fiori app “Manage Launchpad Spaces”, select the already created space ZFI_GL_REPORTS

Now click on  button

List of available pages will show.

Here find our page ZFI_GL_REPORTS

Select this page by clicking checkbox and then click on button

Click on button.

Page is now assigned to Space.

  1. Assign Fiori App / Tile to Section

Access the Fiori App “Manage Launchpad Pages”

Select the page ZFI_GL_REPORTS

Click on  button

Following screen will appear:

Select the Fiori app “Display Line Items in General Ledger”

and click on  button

Now this Fiori tile will show as assigned to page (as shown below)

Click on  button.

This completes the Fiori tile assignment to Page and section.

Now the required app is visible for user on Fiori interface.

Now you can see the tile under the Space and Page.

Just go to User Homepage and find the Space “GL Reports”

Here you can see the Page and Tile under the same Space.

This concludes the article to buidup the Space, Page, Section and Tile/App assignment.

Hope you have enjoyed the read.

Please do comment for any observations and recommendations.

Best Regards,

entry.title: Introducing the new SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library
entry.content:
The SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library has long been the go-to resource for anyone looking to plan and implement SAP Fiori applications. It provides a comprehensive catalog of all available apps, technical configuration details, installation requirements, and direct links to related resources like the Product Availability Matrix and Maintenance Planner.

We are now releasing a new version, rebuilt with a modern, intuitive interface that dramatically reduces the time it takes to find exactly what you need. More robust filtering lets you find adjacent apps so you can ensure an end-to-end SAP Fiori experience across your business processes.

Live now at fal.cloud.sap , built from the ground up with a focus on clarity and productivity, this new version transforms how SAP customers, functional consultants, partners, administrators and developers explore and implement SAP Fiori apps.

See it in action — watch an intro video

 

What's New - New Functionality That Improves Usability and Reduces App Search Time

New Home Page - A Smarter Starting Point That Gets You to Exactly What You're Looking For
On login, you are redirected to a new home page designed to get you to the right apps fast. A prominent search bar lets you query by keyword, while a Product Suite dropdown lets you scope results to SAP S/4HANA, SAP BTP, or other environments before you even search.
The Quick Links section puts your most-used release versions one click away — from the latest editions to specific Feature Package Stacks like and Public Cloud releases. A side panel always keeps Home, Apps, Current Selections, and Saved Selections within reach.

 

A smarter starting point — find, filter, and navigate to your apps instantly
Search Wide, Drill Deep — New Filtering Gets You to the Right Apps Fast

In the third quarter of 2024, we released the new List View for the Fiori Apps Library — and the response has been overwhelmingly positive, with great feedback continuing to come in.

The enhanced Apps List View provides a streamlined interface for searching, filtering, and personalizing the display of available apps by product suite and release version. Use this feature to efficiently find, organize, and customize app information to meet your needs.

By default, the List View displays all available apps at their highest release version. Once you select a Product Suite and Release Version, the list instantly narrows to show only what is available in your environment. The horizontal filters at the top address your primary constraints — keyword search, product suite, and release version — for quick and easy navigation.

For more granular exploration, the vertical filter sidebar on the left enables faceted navigation, allowing you to drill down by UI Technology (Fiori, SAP GUI, SAPUI5, WebDynpro), Line of Business (Finance, Supply Chain, Sourcing, etc.), Role, and more.

Start broad, drill deep — layered filters surface the right apps
The two complementary filter layers that work in harmony to help you zero in on exactly the right apps. The horizontal filter bar lets you set your broad selection criteria — such as Product Suite, Release Version, or Line of Business — instantly scoping the entire library to your context (for example, filtering by Finance surfaces 4900 relevant apps). The vertical facet panel then lets you refine further within those results — revealing that 1,014 of those Finance apps also belong specifically to Budget and Finance — without ever losing sight of the broader picture. Together, these two filter levels give you a powerful, layered approach to app discovery: start wide, drill deep, and arrive at a precise, meaningful shortlist in just a few clicks.

All the App Details You Need, Organised So You Can Find Them Without the Effort

The Detail View provides comprehensive information about an app — including its role, product suite, release details, app ID, features, and related apps — organized across several tabs. These cover important SAP Notes, installation requirements (front-end and back-end components), configuration requirements (SAPUI5 applications, OData services, and Fiori Launchpad data), business catalogs, groups, spaces, pages, business roles, as well as extensibility and support information. Together, these details help you assess an app's suitability and plan its implementation effectively.

Everything you need to assess and implement an app — now clearly organized

An Enhanced Selection Experience That Remembers What You're Working On — Every Time

The App Selection feature introduces a clear separation between app discovery and selection, while automatically saving your choices and preserving context throughout your session. It allows you to curate a specific list of applications for implementation, planning, or mass configuration — with selections carried over automatically across user sessions.

You can manage selections individually by using the toggle switch in the Selected column of the apps list view table, or in bulk using the Select All or Deselect All buttons at the top right of the table header. To focus exclusively on your chosen applications, click the Selected (n) tab to filter out all non-selected items.

Selection toggle for app

Current Selections, Always at Hand

Current Selection accessible from side bar, shows a consolidated view of all the apps you have selected across various places within the SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library — for example, while browsing the apps list or search results. The key functionality here is the ability to filter this accumulated selection by Product Suite and Release Version, which lets you quickly check whether your chosen apps are available or not available in a specific release.

 

Current Selection shows all your chosen apps — filter by suite and release to confirm availability instantly
Saved Selections - Save Once, Reuse Anytime

Saved Selections also accessible from side bar, allows users to save their current selection under a named list for future reference, making it easy to revisit, load, or share curated sets of apps without having to re-select them each time. A particularly powerful feature visible here is the "Upload App Selection" option in the top right corner, which allows users to import a list of apps directly from a spreadsheet file.

 

Saved selections let you revisit, load, or share curated app sets across sessions
Work in the Language You're Most Comfortable In — Across 10 UI and 40 Content Languages

The library is available in 10 UI languages, configurable via the app or browser settings, with all search and filter categories — including roles, catalogs, and system objects — displayed in your selected language. For localized content, a separate Content Language setting supporting approximately 40 languages allows app metadata such as role names, business catalog titles, and app launcher subtitles to be displayed in your preferred language, making the library accessible and practical for global teams.

Choose your UI language and a separate content language for localized app metadata

What's Coming

Some features, such as Aggregate and Upgrade Analysis, are still making their way to the new version — in the meantime, they remain accessible via the classic library, with seamless navigation between the two. We are committed to continuously enhancing the new library, adding missing functionality and incorporating your feedback along the way.

Getting Started

Navigate to fal.cloud.sap and sign in using your SAP Universal ID, S-User, or P-User. Access to the SAP Fiori Apps Library is restricted to users authenticated via a SAP  Universal ID. If you don't have a Universal ID yet, you can register directly from the start page. Once signed in, your saved selections, view variants, and personalization settings are automatically tied to your account.

Explore and share your feedback.

entry.title: SAP Fiori Development Newsletter issue #40 (May 2026)
entry.content:
The mid-year conference season is upon us with many opportunities for you to meet our development teams and have conversations with your peers about the topics you care about most. Some of our upcoming events include:

  • SAP Sapphire in Orlando on May 11-13
  • SAP UX Innovation Day in Palo Alto on June 11
  • UI5con in St. Leon-Rot on July 14

Join us to learn about using AI to accelerate development, MCP servers, and the latest UX strategy updates from SAP

If you want to join other developers to discuss various traditional and AI-powered Fiori development-related topics from the comfort of your office or home, I encourage you to join our monthly SAP Fiori development roundtable. Register online or email me for an invitation.

Development News

SAP Fiori elements feature map: what is available in each SAPUI5 version

If you work with SAP Fiori elements, you have probably run into the problem where it is hard to see in which UI5 version a specific feature was introduced. Marian Zeis created a feature map that shows when a feature first appears. It’s updated automatically whenever new versions are released. Explore the feature map.

Key User Adaptation: powerful new features and seamless transition to Developer Extensibility

With key user adaptation, you can change the user interface of SAP Fiori apps directly in the Fiori launchpad. The goal of key user extensibility is to empower business users to be able to adapt their working environment to suit their needs, without having to rely on system administrators. Learn more.

SAP Fiori tools 2603 update: RAP Project Accelerator, walkthrough, adaptation improvements, and more

SAP Fiori tools 2603 delivers improvements across development transparency, onboarding, AI-supported development, and extensibility. Highlights include Project Accelerator support for RAP, a change in navigation from properties to its source, a new walkthrough in Visual Studio Code, and the ability to import existing key user changes into an SAPUI5 Adaptation Project. See details.

Introducing UI5 plugins for Claude: AI-powered development for SAPUI5 and OpenUI5

UI5 plugins for Claude enhance UI5 development workflows with AI assistance by embedding current, maintained guidelines directly into the development workflow. The result is that Claude Code provides UI5-accurate suggestions without you having to paste documentation into every conversation. Learn more.

How to migrate UI5 projects to the latest UI5 best practices

This blog post focuses on how UI5 MCP server can support you in migrating your UI5 project easily to the latest best practices with the help of GitHub Copilot Coding Agent. Migrate your project.

Declarative UI Integration Cards - A consistent framework with flexible content

UI Integration Cards are utilized in many SAP solutions. A new blog post shows key qualities of declarative Integration Cards: They offer a range of display options and minimalist interactions, including List Card, Table Card, Timeline, Calendar, Analytical, and Object Card with input elements. Read more.

More power, new name: The UI Flexibility Service explained

SAPUI5 flexibility allows developers, key users, and end users to adapt standard apps with simple drag-and-drop tools. Since it’s not only for key users, we renamed this capability to UI flexibility service instead of the older, potentially confusing name UI5 flexibility for key users. Learn more.

Navigating from an SAP Fiori elements application to another application

While the SAPUI5 documentation explains how to navigate to other applications in detail, the code snippets sometimes make it difficult to visualize how each navigation pattern behaves in the UI. Mio Yasutake created a sample repository that demonstrates various Fiori elements navigation patterns along with their actual UI behavior. See how.

Events

SAP UX Innovation Days are back for 2026

SAP UX Innovation Day Silicon Valley – Thursday, June 11
